message.content:
You are an INDEPENDENT verifier adjudicating audit finding GitHub issue #9 for /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay. You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. Run repros with /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git, no gh.

FINDING (#9, stated severity MEDIUM): src/assay_engine/orchestration/phases.py:13-26 — two defects: (a) discovery-only studies cannot legally reach REPORT because can_advance_to allows only strictly value+1, so a pure-discovery run can't get past CONFIRM(5) through ADJUDICATE(6)/SCORE(7) to REPORT(8); (b) no mode↔phase coupling — nothing prevents a discovery-only study from entering the external-claims-only ADJUDICATE/SCORE phases.

Independently verify: Phase.CONFIRM.can_advance_to(Phase.REPORT) — is it False? grep StudyMode in phases.py. Consider counter-arguments (the orchestration graph that wires legal edges is explicitly "wired later" — is this premature? Is sub-claim (a) a genuine correctness bug or just an artifact of an unfinished phase model?). Decide TRUE/PARTIAL/FALSE per sub-claim. Assess MEDIUM severity.

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high | medium | low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding independently. Let me start by reading the target file.

content.content:
1	"""Phase machine — the ordered stages a study moves through.
2	
3	The ordering encodes the method: ingest and build the *blind* baseline before any external
4	claim is touched (Firewall A), and discover before confirm (Firewall B). Gates guard the
5	transitions between these phases.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	from enum import Enum
11	
12	
13	class Phase(Enum):
14	    """Ordered analysis phases. Value is the sort key used to forbid backward transitions."""
15	
16	    INGEST = 1            # raw source → canonical corpus
17	    BASELINE = 2          # build the independent baseline, blind to any claims (Firewall A)
18	    DISCOVERY = 3         # data surfaces candidate hypotheses
19	    PREREGISTER = 4       # lock + timestamp hypotheses before confirmation
20	    CONFIRM = 5           # confirmatory tests on held-out / null distributions (Firewall B)
21	    ADJUDICATE = 6        # convert external claims to verdicts against the blind baseline
22	    SCORE = 7             # score the external source against the validated baseline
23	    REPORT = 8            # assemble the reproducibility package
24	
25	    def can_advance_to(self, other: "Phase") -> bool:
26	        return other.value == self.value + 1
27

# walk discovery-only path: can we get from CONFIRM to REPORT skipping ADJUDICATE/SCORE?
print('Any single-step jump CONFIRM->REPORT:', Phase.CONFIRM.can_advance_to(Phase.REPORT))
"
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && grep -rn "StudyMode" --include="*.py" . | head -50; echo "---phases.py StudyMode---"; grep -n "StudyMode\|mode" src/assay_engine/orchestration/phases.py

content.content:
1	"""Study definition — the adapter's research questions and pre-registration handle.
2	
3	A study definition binds an adapter's pieces together for one concrete run: which parser,
4	which mode, the optional claims source, any extra feature builders, and the research
5	questions. It lives in the instance repository (one repo per study, ADR-0002), not in the
6	engine. The engine reads it to drive the run and to anchor pre-registration.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	from dataclasses import dataclass
12	from enum import Enum
13	from typing import Sequence
14	
15	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ExternalClaimsSource
16	from assay_engine.contracts.features import FeatureBuilder
17	from assay_engine.contracts.parser import IngestionParser
18	
19	
20	class StudyMode(Enum):
21	    """The two modes from the charter. A study may combine both."""
22	
23	    DISCOVERY = "discovery"
24	    ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS = "adjudicate_external_claims"
25	
26	
27	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
28	class StudyDefinition:
29	    """Everything the engine needs to run one study, supplied by an adapter."""
30	
31	    name: str
32	    modes: frozenset[StudyMode]
33	    parser: IngestionParser
34	    research_questions: tuple[str, ...]
35	    feature_builders: tuple[FeatureBuilder, ...] = ()
36	    claims_source: ExternalClaimsSource | None = None
37	
38	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
39	        if not self.modes:
40	            raise ValueError("StudyDefinition must declare at least one mode")
41	        needs_claims = StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS in self.modes
42	        if needs_claims and self.claims_source is None:
43	            raise ValueError(
44	                "Adjudication mode requires a claims_source; none was provided"
45	            )
46	        if not needs_claims and self.claims_source is not None:
47	            # A discovery-only study must not carry a claims source — it would risk
48	            # leaking external judgments into a pipeline that is meant to be blind.
49	            raise ValueError(
50	                "claims_source supplied but ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS mode not declared"
51	            )
52	
53	    @staticmethod
54	    def discovery(
55	        name: str,
56	        parser: IngestionParser,
57	        research_questions: Sequence[str],
58	        feature_builders: Sequence[FeatureBuilder] = (),
59	    ) -> "StudyDefinition":
60	        return StudyDefinition(
61	            name=name,
62	            modes=frozenset({StudyMode.DISCOVERY}),
63	            parser=parser,
64	            research_questions=tuple(research_questions),
65	            feature_builders=tuple(feature_builders),
66	        )
67

content.text: Let me read the gates.py and orchestration __init__ to understand how phases are wired and whether there's an orchestration graph.

content.content:
1	"""Governance gates — deterministic, code-enforced transition checks.
2	
3	A gate guards a transition between phases. It evaluates code-enforced preconditions (the
4	firewalls, lock state, provenance recorded) and, where the governance model requires it,
5	records a human approval. A gate cannot be silently bypassed: the transition is only legal
6	if the gate returns an ``approved`` decision, and the engine records the decision to the
7	append-only provenance trail before the next phase runs.
8	
9	The concrete gate set and the human-in-the-loop wiring are lifted from the prior platform's
10	governance graph; this module fixes the gate contract.
11	"""
12	
13	from __future__ import annotations
14	
15	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
16	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping
17	
18	from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase
19	
20	
21	class GateError(RuntimeError):
22	    """Raised when a transition is attempted through a gate that did not approve it."""
23	
24	
25	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
26	class GateDecision:
27	    approved: bool
28	    gate: str
29	    reason: str
30	    evidence: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
31	
32	
33	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
34	class Gate:
35	    """A guard on the transition ``frm -> to``.
36	
37	    ``precondition`` is a pure, code-enforced check over the run context returning a
38	    :class:`GateDecision`. ``requires_human`` marks gates that additionally need a recorded
39	    operator approval (handled by the orchestration layer when wired).
40	    """
41	
42	    name: str
43	    frm: Phase
44	    to: Phase
45	    precondition: Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], GateDecision]
46	    requires_human: bool = False
47	
48	    def evaluate(self, context: Mapping[str, Any]) -> GateDecision:
49	        if not self.frm.can_advance_to(self.to):
50	            raise GateError(
51	                f"gate {self.name!r} declares an illegal transition "
52	                f"{self.frm.name} -> {self.to.name}"
53	            )
54	        decision = self.precondition(context)
55	        if not decision.approved:
56	            raise GateError(f"gate {self.name!r} blocked transition: {decision.reason}")
57	        return decision
58

1	"""Orchestration & governance gates — the analysis graph and its human-in-the-loop gates.
2	
3	The phase machine advances a study through its structural transitions; deterministic,
4	code-enforced gates sit at those transitions and cannot be silently bypassed (GOVERNANCE.md).
5	The graph itself is built on LangGraph/LangChain and lifted from the prior platform.
6	"""
7	
8	from assay_engine.orchestration.gates import Gate, GateDecision, GateError
9	from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase
10	
11	__all__ = ["Gate", "GateDecision", "GateError", "Phase"]
12

content.text:
I now have everything needed to adjudicate. The orchestration graph that would wire legal edges does not exist yet (it's described as built on LangGraph and "lifted from the prior platform" — i.e., future work). There is no graph, no mode-to-phase coupling code anywhere.

Let me verify my reasoning on sub-claim (a): is `can_advance_to` actually the gating mechanism for run progression, or just a backward-transition guard?

The docstring on `can_advance_to` says "Value is the sort key used to **forbid backward transitions**." But the implementation is stricter than that name implies — it requires *exactly* `value+1`, not just `value > self.value`. So it forbids both backward AND skip-ahead transitions. The `Gate.evaluate` uses it as a hard legality check that raises `GateError`.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
from src.assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase
# Is there ANY legal path CONFIRM->REPORT that skips ADJUDICATE/SCORE? Only adjacent steps legal.
print('CONFIRM->SCORE:', Phase.CONFIRM.can_advance_to(Phase.SCORE))
print('CONFIRM->REPORT:', Phase.CONFIRM.can_advance_to(Phase.REPORT))
print('SCORE->REPORT:', Phase.SCORE.can_advance_to(Phase.REPORT))
print('ADJUDICATE->REPORT:', Phase.ADJUDICATE.can_advance_to(Phase.REPORT))
# So the only path to REPORT is via SCORE->REPORT, and the only path to SCORE is ADJUDICATE->SCORE.
# A discovery-only study must traverse ADJUDICATE and SCORE to reach REPORT.
"

content.content:
CONFIRM->SCORE: False
CONFIRM->REPORT: False
SCORE->REPORT: True
ADJUDICATE->REPORT: False

content.text:
Verification complete. My findings:

**Sub-claim (a) — discovery-only studies cannot legally reach REPORT:** Confirmed at the code level. `can_advance_to` returns `True` only for `value == self.value + 1`. The single-step chain is the *only* legal route, so the only path to REPORT(8) is SCORE(7)→REPORT, the only path to SCORE is ADJUDICATE(6)→SCORE. A discovery-only run is therefore forced through ADJUDICATE and SCORE — the external-claims phases — to terminate. There is no skip edge.

**Sub-claim (b) — no mode↔phase coupling:** Confirmed. Grep finds zero references to `StudyMode`, `modes`, or `DISCOVERY` anywhere in the orchestration package. Nothing consults the study's declared modes when deciding which phases/gates apply. `StudyMode` lives only in `contracts/study.py` and tests.

**Counter-argument (the strongest refutation):** The orchestration graph that wires concrete legal edges is explicitly deferred — `__init__.py` says the graph "is built on LangGraph/LangChain and lifted from the prior platform," and `gates.py` says human wiring is "handled by the orchestration layer when wired." So the linear phase model may be an intentionally-unfinished skeleton, and the real edge set + mode coupling would be supplied by the not-yet-written graph. This is a genuine mitigating factor: `Phase.can_advance_to` may be intended only as a structural backward-guard, with the graph (not the enum) defining which phases a given study actually visits.

However, this counter-argument does not fully neutralize the finding. Two points stand against it:
- `Gate.evaluate` *already* calls `self.frm.can_advance_to(self.to)` as a hard legality check that raises `GateError` on any non-adjacent transition. So `can_advance_to` is wired today as a real gate — not a passive sort key. The future graph would have to either avoid this method or it would reject a discovery study's CONFIRM→REPORT edge.
- The method's own docstring claims it exists to "forbid backward transitions," but it also forbids forward skips. That gap between documented intent (backward-only) and behavior (exactly-+1) is the actual defect for (a), and it exists in committed code now.

**Severity:** The original MEDIUM is slightly high for current impact. No run driver consumes this yet (no graph exists), so nothing breaks at runtime today — this is a latent design defect, not an active bug. But it is a real correctness trap that will bite the moment the graph is wired against the existing `Gate.evaluate` check, and (b) is a genuine firewall-relevant gap (a blind discovery study being routed through external-claims phases would violate the project's stated firewall premise). I assess LOW-to-MEDIUM.

VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE
CONFIDENCE: high
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: Slightly overstated. Both sub-claims are factually correct in committed code, but neither has runtime impact yet because no graph/run-driver consumes the phase machine. It is a latent design defect that will activate when the orchestration graph is wired against the already-live `Gate.evaluate` legality check. Realistic severity LOW-MEDIUM rather than MEDIUM. Sub-claim (b) is the more substantive concern given the firewall/blindness premise; (a) is partly an artifact of an unfinished phase model but is nonetheless a documented-intent-vs-behavior mismatch (docstring says "forbid backward transitions," code also forbids forward skips).
REASONING: Empirically, `Phase.CONFIRM.can_advance_to(Phase.REPORT)` is False; `can_advance_to` returns True only for `other.value == self.value + 1` (phases.py:26). The sole legal route to REPORT(8) is SCORE(7)→REPORT and to SCORE is ADJUDICATE(6)→SCORE, so any study — including discovery-only — must traverse the external-claims phases ADJUDICATE/SCORE to terminate; no skip edge exists (a confirmed). `grep StudyMode` in phases.py and the whole orchestration package returns nothing — modes are never consulted to scope phases or gates; coupling lives nowhere (b confirmed). `Gate.evaluate` (gates.py:49) already uses `can_advance_to` as a hard, exception-raising legality check, so the stricter-than-documented behavior is live, weakening the "premature / will be fixed by the graph" defense. The "wired later" graph (LangGraph, per __init__.py) genuinely does not exist, which caps current impact and is the strongest mitigating counter-argument.
FIX_GUIDANCE: (a) Either relax `can_advance_to` to `other.value > self.value` and let the (future) explicit edge set define adjacency, or — better — replace the value-arithmetic adjacency with an explicit per-phase successor map so legal transitions are data, not arithmetic; this also lets discovery-only studies define CONFIRM→REPORT (or DISCOVERY/PREREGISTER→REPORT) directly. (b) Introduce mode↔phase coupling: derive the set of required phases from `StudyDefinition.modes` (DISCOVERY skips ADJUDICATE/SCORE; ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS includes them) and have the orchestration layer / gate set honor it, ideally with a test asserting a discovery-only study never enters ADJUDICATE/SCORE. Until the graph lands, at minimum fix the docstring or the method to match intent so `Gate.evaluate` does not silently encode an unintended skip-prohibition.
